12 min read
Just now

--

Before we dive into algorithms, neural networks, and Python code, let’s answer a more fundamental question: Why do we need machine learning in the first place?
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Introduction
Imagine you’re building an email application. One of its most important features is filtering spam messages before they reach the user’s inbox.
At first, the solution seems straightforward.
You could write a set of rules like these:

• If the email contains the phrase “Congratulations! You won!”, mark it as spam.

• If it contains “Claim your prize now!”, mark it as spam.

• If the sender appears on a blacklist, mark it as spam.

• If the same message is sent to thousands of recipients, mark it as spam.
This approach works… at least for a while.
But then spammers change their tactics.
Instead of writing:
Congratulations! You won!
They write:
Congratulati0ns! You w0n!
Or perhaps:
Your package is waiting. Click here.
Each day, new tricks appear.
Soon, you find yourself adding more and more rules.
Hundreds of them.
Then thousands.
Eventually, maintaining the system becomes nearly impossible.
This is where machine learning enters the picture.
Instead of telling the computer how to recognize spam, we let it learn what spam looks like by studying thousands of real examples.
That simple change transforms how computers solve problems.

Traditional Programming
Press enter or click to view image in full size

AI-generated imageBefore understanding machine learning, it’s important to understand how computers have traditionally been programmed.
In traditional programming, a computer follows instructions that humans explicitly write.
Think of a recipe.
If a recipe says:

• Crack two eggs.

• Add flour.

• Mix for two minutes.

• Bake at 180°C for 30 minutes.
You don’t invent your own steps — you simply follow the instructions.
Computers behave the same way.
For example, suppose you want a program that determines whether someone is old enough to vote.
The logic is simple:
If age ≥ 18
Eligible to vote
Else
Not eligibleThe programmer knows the rule.
The computer simply executes it.
This approach works extremely well for problems with clear, well-defined rules.
Here are a few examples:

• Calculating taxes

• Computing a student’s average grade

• Sorting names alphabetically

• Checking whether a password meets certain requirements
For these tasks, the programmer already knows the exact steps needed to produce the correct answer.
We can summarize traditional programming like this:
Rules + Data → Answers
The programmer supplies the rules, and the computer applies those rules to the data.
Simple.
Predictable.
Reliable.
So why don’t we use this approach for everything?

When Rules Become Impossible to Write
Let’s imagine you’re building software that can recognize cats in photographs.
At first, it sounds easy.
You begin writing rules.
If the image has two ears,
it's a cat.Unfortunately…
Dogs have two ears.
Foxes have two ears.
Even rabbits have two ears.
So you improve the rule.
Two ears
+
Whiskers
+
TailBetter.
But what if the cat is sleeping?
What if the ears are hidden?
What if only half of the cat appears in the picture?
What if it's a black cat in a dark room?
What if the image is blurry?
Suddenly, every new situation requires another exception.
You continue adding rules.
Then more.
And more.
Eventually, you realize something important:
There are simply too many possibilities to describe manually.
Humans can instantly recognize a cat because our brains have learned from seeing thousands of examples throughout our lives.
We never sat down and memorized a list of 10,000 rules describing every possible cat.
We learned through experience.
What if computers could do the same?

A Different Way of Thinking
Machine learning flips traditional programming upside down.
Instead of giving the computer the rules… we give it examples.
Imagine you collect 100,000 photos.
Some are labeled: Cat
Others are labeled: Not Cat
You don't explain what ears look like.
You don't describe whiskers.
You never define tails.
Instead, you simply show the computer example after example after example.
Over time, it begins discovering patterns on its own.
It notices that certain shapes frequently appear together.
It learns textures, colors, proportions.
Eventually, when it encounters a brand-new image it has never seen before, it can often determine whether the picture contains a cat.
Nobody explicitly programmed those rules.
The computer learned them.
This is the key idea behind machine learning.
Instead of programming the rules, we let the computer discover the rules from data.
This single idea powers many of the intelligent systems we use every day.

Traditional Programming vs. Machine Learning
Let’s compare the two approaches side by side.
Traditional Programming
You already know the rules.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Traditional ProgrammingA Simple Example
Suppose we want to determine whether a person is old enough to vote. The rule is straightforward: if someone is 18 or older, they are eligible to vote.
Rule:
if age >= 18:
eligible_to_vote = True
else:
eligible_to_vote = FalseData:
age = 21The computer combines the rule with the data.

• Rule: A person is eligible if they are at least 18 years old.

• Data: The person’s age is 21.
The computer evaluates the rule:
21 >= 18which is True, so the output is:
eligible_to_vote = TrueMachine Learning
You don’t know the rules.
Instead, you provide examples.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Machine Learning — TrainingOnce the model has learned these rules, it can make predictions on completely new data.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Machine Learning — PredictionA Simple Example
Suppose we want to build a system that can recognize whether an image contains a cat.
Unlike the voting example, we don’t know the exact rules for identifying a cat. Instead, we provide the computer with many labeled examples.
Training Data:
| Image | Correct Answer |
| ----- | -------------- |
| 🐱 | Cat |
| 🐱 | Cat |
| 🐱 | Cat |
| 🐶 | Not Cat |
| 🐰 | Not Cat |
| 🦊 | Not Cat |These examples are fed into a machine learning algorithm, which analyzes the images and discovers patterns that distinguish cats from other animals.
The result is a trained model.
Making a Prediction:
Once the model has been trained, we can give it a completely new image that it has never seen before.
New Image 🐱
↓
Learned Model
↓
Prediction
Cat ✅The model compares what it learned during training with the new image and predicts the most likely answer.
Traditional programming depends on human knowledge.
Machine learning depends on data.
That doesn’t mean machine learning replaces traditional programming. In fact, most modern software combines both. An online shopping website, for example, still uses traditional code to process payments, manage user accounts, and display products. Machine learning is added only for tasks where writing explicit rules is difficult, such as recommending products or detecting fraudulent transactions.
Understanding when to use each approach is one of the first steps toward thinking like a machine learning engineer or data scientist.

What Does It Mean for a Machine to Learn?
When people first hear the term machine learning, it’s easy to imagine a computer that thinks like a human. In reality, machines don’t “understand” the world the way we do.
A machine doesn't know what a cat is.
It doesn't know what spam means.
It doesn't know why someone might default on a loan.
All it sees are numbers.
So what does “learning” actually mean?
Let’s use a simple example.
Imagine you’re trying to predict the price of a house.
You collect data from hundreds of homes, and each one includes information such as:
Size (m²) | Bedrooms | Age | Price |
----------|----------|-----|----------|
80 | 2 | 10 | $420,000 |
120 | 3 | 5 | $610,000 |
150 | 4 | 2 | $760,000 |When a new house comes along, you want the computer to estimate its price.
The machine doesn’t magically know the answer.
Instead, it starts with a guess.
Maybe it predicts: $300,000
But the correct price is: $420,000
The prediction is wrong.
The machine measures how wrong it was.
Then it adjusts its internal parameters so that next time, it makes a slightly better prediction.
It repeats this process again.
And again.
And again.
Thousands — or even millions — of times.
Each mistake teaches the model something new.
Eventually, its predictions become much more accurate.
This is what learning means in machine learning.
Learning is the process of improving predictions by finding patterns in data.
Notice something important.
The machine isn’t memorizing every house it has ever seen.
Instead, it’s discovering relationships like:

• Larger houses usually cost more.

• Newer houses often have higher prices.

• More bedrooms generally increase value.
These aren’t rules that a programmer wrote.
They’re patterns the model discovered by analyzing many examples.

A Student Learning for an Exam
One of the best ways to understand machine learning is to compare it to studying for an exam.
Imagine two students.
Student A
Student A memorizes every answer from last year’s exam.
When the teacher asks the exact same questions, the student performs perfectly.
But if the teacher changes the wording slightly, the student becomes confused.
The student memorized.
They didn’t truly understand.
Student B
Student B studies the underlying concepts.
Instead of memorizing answers, they learn the principles behind them.
Now, even if the exam contains brand-new questions, the student can still solve them.
This is what we want from a machine learning model.
A good model doesn’t simply remember the training data.
It learns patterns that allow it to make accurate predictions on data it has never seen before.
This ability is called generalization, and it’s one of the most important ideas in machine learning.
We’ll explore it in much more detail later in this series when we discuss overfitting and underfitting.

Where Do We Use Machine Learning?
Machine learning is already part of your daily life — even if you don’t realize it.
Every time you unlock your phone with your face…
Machine learning.
When Netflix recommends a movie you end up loving…
Machine learning.
When your bank detects a suspicious transaction before you do…
Machine learning.
When your email automatically filters spam…
Machine learning.
Let’s look at a few more examples.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Real-World ApplicationsHealthcare
Hospitals use machine learning to help doctors identify diseases from medical images, estimate a patient’s risk of developing certain conditions, and recommend personalized treatment plans.
It’s important to note that these systems usually assist medical professionals rather than replace them.
Finance
Banks analyze millions of transactions every day.
Instead of writing thousands of rules to identify fraud, they train models using historical transaction data.
The model learns what normal behavior looks like and flags unusual activity for further review.
Retail and E-commerce
Have you ever wondered how online stores seem to know exactly what you might want to buy next?
Recommendation systems analyze previous purchases, browsing history, and the behavior of similar customers to suggest products you’re likely to be interested in.
Transportation
Navigation apps estimate travel times by analyzing historical traffic patterns, current road conditions, accidents, and even weather.
Ride-sharing platforms use machine learning to estimate demand and optimize driver assignments.
Manufacturing
Factories use sensors to monitor equipment continuously.
Rather than waiting for a machine to fail, predictive maintenance models estimate when maintenance should be performed, reducing downtime and saving money.
Entertainment
Streaming platforms recommend movies, TV shows, and music based on your viewing ha
